mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the full survey of the solar system (outline path A), including all major constituents — planets, dwarf planets, asteroids, Kuiper belt, Oort cloud, comets, meteoroids, and the heliosphere — as well as formation theories and comparison with extrasolar systems (A.6).

Directly covers all aspects of outline section B: photosphere, chromosphere, corona (B.1), solar magnetic activity, sunspots, solar cycle (B.2), interior structure and energy generation (B.3), electromagnetic output across all wavelengths (B.4), and solar-terrestrial relationships (B.5).

Comprehensively covers outline section E: physical characteristics, orbital mechanics, mass and gravitational field, surface features (craters, maria, rilles), origin and evolution, chemical composition of lunar rock samples, and the Sun-Earth-Moon system including eclipses (E.7.b).

Covers the Earth as a planet (outline section D): orbital motion and rotation (D.2), physical properties including magnetosphere (D.4), internal structure (D.5), and origin of Earth and its envelopes (D.6); also addresses Earth as one of the major planets (A.2).

Directly covers Mercury (C.1.a), the Solar System's innermost terrestrial planet, with detail on its heavily cratered surface, spin-orbit resonance, thin magnetic field, and extreme temperature swings.
Directly covers Venus (C.1.b), including its dense CO₂ atmosphere, retrograde rotation, volcanic surface, absence of magnetic field, and history of space exploration.
Directly covers Mars (C.1.d), including its surface geology (Olympus Mons, Valles Marineris), hemispheric dichotomy, two moons, past oceans, and its status as a primary target for crewed exploration.
Directly covers Jupiter (C.3.a), including its hydrogen-helium atmosphere, Great Red Spot, metallic hydrogen interior, strong magnetic field, and the Galilean moons (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto).
Directly covers Saturn (C.3.b), including its prominent ring system, layered interior, magnetic field, and its 274+ moons including Titan — the only Solar System moon with a substantial atmosphere.
Directly covers Uranus (C.3.c), an ice giant with an extreme 98° axial tilt, offset magnetosphere, ring system, and 28 known moons, explored only by Voyager 2.

Directly covers Neptune (C.3.d), the Solar System's outermost known planet, including its active storm systems, offset magnetosphere, fragmented ring system, and Triton as a captured Kuiper Belt object.
Directly covers minor planets/asteroids (A.3.a and C.2), including main-belt, Jupiter Trojan, and near-Earth populations, spectral classification, and impact hazard history.

Directly covers comets (A.3.b): icy bodies that develop coma and tails, short-period origins in the Kuiper Belt, long-period origins in the Oort Cloud, and their role in meteor showers and organic compound delivery to early Earth.

Covers the distribution and properties of natural satellites across the solar system (A.4), including formation mechanisms (accretion, capture, giant impact), tidal locking, and the diversity of geological activity among moons.

Covers the major planets of the solar system as a class (A.2) and their characteristics including axial tilt, atmosphere, magnetosphere, and orbital clearing; also connects to satellite distribution (A.4) and the contested definition of planet vs. dwarf planet.

Covers Earth's orbital period (the year) at outline path D.2, and the use of Earth's orbital cycle as the astronomical basis of chronology (E.7.a.ii), including the tropical, sidereal, and Julian year definitions.

Covers the Earth's rotation period (the day) as defined at D.2, and the astronomical basis of chronology including the sidereal and solar day (E.7.a.ii), with connections to rotational irregularities and leap seconds.

Covers the effects of Earth's axial tilt and orbital position on seasonal variation (D.2), which is the primary driver of the seasonal cycle discussed in this article, fitting the outline's treatment of Earth's orbital effects.

Covers the origin and evolution of Earth's atmosphere (D.6) and solar-terrestrial interactions including the greenhouse effect and ozone, relevant to solar radiation's effects (B.5); also addresses the atmosphere as part of D.4 (Earth's physical properties).

Relevant to the volcanic theory of the origin of Moon's surface features (E.4.c) and Moon's volcanic history (E.4.b); also covers volcanism on Venus (C.1.b), which compares to Earth's volcanic activity in the context of terrestrial planets.

Covers gravitational interactions that govern planetary distances (A.4), gravitational perturbations among bodies in the solar system (A.5), and the orbital mechanics of Earth's revolution around the Sun (D.2).
Covers the full electromagnetic spectrum, directly relevant to solar radiation (B.4), which the outline specifies includes light, radio waves, and particles emitted by the Sun.

Covers stellar evolution — including the life cycles of main-sequence stars like the Sun — directly relevant to the Sun's interior evolution (B.3) and to understanding the Sun's origin and its eventual fate, contextualizing origin theories (A.6).

Galileo's telescopic discoveries directly underlie knowledge of Jupiter's moons (C.3.a), the phases of Venus (C.1.b), and sunspot observations (B.2.b), making him a historically essential contributor to solar system science.

Classical (Newtonian) mechanics underlies the regularities of planetary distances (A.4), gravitational perturbations (A.5), and the orbital and rotational dynamics of Earth (D.2) that are fundamental to solar system science.

Newton's law of universal gravitation directly explains the regularities of planetary distances and distribution of satellites (A.4), gravitational perturbations among solar system bodies (A.5), and Earth's orbital and rotational dynamics (D.2).
Astronomy is the discipline through which the solar system is studied; the article covers planetary science and solar astronomy as subfields directly relevant to the survey of the solar system (A).
Covers the physics of magnetic fields underpinning solar magnetic activity and localized strong magnetic fields at the Sun's surface (B.2.a), the sunspot cycle (B.2.b), and Earth's magnetism (D.4).
Covers the interplanetary medium (A.3.d) as part of its description of outer space regions including the near-vacuum, cosmic rays, magnetic fields, and the heliosphere between planetary bodies.
